data.title: Takashimaya Reports More Than 8% Increase in January Sales
data.description:
Takashimaya Co. reported an 8.4% increase in overall sales for January, according to a Feb. 14 filing.

Including the company's three regional stores in Okayama and Takasaki, sales increased 6.3% year over year, the filing said.

The company's department store sales saw positive growth in most locations, with sales in Kyoto and Rakusai, and Shinjuku stores rising 18.4% and 14.5%, respectively, on year.

However, Sakai and Senboku stores saw declines of 0.9% and 3.1%, respectively.

The items that saw the most sales in the three regional stores were personal items, household goods and other.

data.title: Invinity Partners Frontier Power to Deploy 2 GWh Energy Storage in UK
data.description:
Invinity Energy Systems said Tuesday it entered a supply agreement with Frontier Power to deploy 2 gigawatt hours of its vanadium flow batteries in projects bidding for a long duration energy storage cap and floor scheme in the UK.

The application window for the scheme is expected to open between the second and third quarters. Frontier will reserve Invinity's manufacturing capacity rights on a first-refusal basis, the UK-based utility-grade energy storage manufacturer said.

Under the deal, Frontier will act as the lead developer and secure financing, land, and permits. The partnership also plans to explore collaborations in Japan, Korea, the US and the EU markets.

data.title: FTX Begins Payouts to Creditors Under $50K: Recovery Could Reach $16B
data.description:
FTX has started repaying creditors today (Tuesday), beginning with those in the “Convenience Class.” This group includes creditors with claims of $50,000 or less.

These individuals will receive full repayment along with 9% annual interest accrued since November 2022. FTX has partnered with BitGo and Kraken to process the payments. Funds are expected to reach recipients within one to three business days after distribution begins.

FTX Starts Creditor Payments Under Reorganization Plan

Eligible creditors must complete KYC verification and submit tax forms to receive payments. Those who miss the deadline can still qualify for later distributions.

Creditors with claims exceeding $50,000 will receive payments starting in the second quarter of 2025. FTX plans to distribute $17 billion in total, with $7 billion allocated for the first phase, CCN reported.

The repayments are part of FTX’s reorganization plan, which took effect on January 3, 2025. The plan follows a prolonged bankruptcy process and asset recovery efforts.

Creditor Recovery Estimates Show 123% Return

The FTX Creditor Recovery Summary outlines total claims of $11.75 billion across various creditor groups. Recovery rates vary, with secured and customer claims ranging from 100% to 142%. Estimated total recoveries stand between $14.46 billion and $16.25 billion, resulting in an overall recovery rate of 123% to 138%.

Dotcom and US customer claims show higher recovery rates, while administrative and secured claims are fully covered. Variations in total recovery stem from factors such as postpetition interest and remission fund recoveries. Some claim groups could receive more than their original claims.

If repayments include cryptocurrency and creditors sell their holdings, it could add selling pressure to the market. However, most repayments are expected in cash, which may limit the impact on FTX’s native token, FTT.

data.title: Bulgaria Current Account Swings to Deficit
data.description:
Bulgaria posted a current account deficit of €459.2 million in December 2024, shifting from a surplus of €345.4 million in the same month of the previous year.

This reversal was largely driven by an expansion in the deficit for goods, which grew to €724 million from €554.7 million in December 2023.

Additionally, the primary income deficit widened to €268.8 million, compared to €264.3 million in the previous year.

On the other hand, the surpluses for both services and secondary income saw a decline, with the service surplus narrowing to €448.9 million from €597 million, and the secondary income surplus falling to €84.7 million from €567.4 million.

data.title: India stocks, rupee, swaps, call at 3:30 p.m. IST
data.description:
STOCKS:

The benchmark BSE Sensex closed marginally lower at 75,967.39, while the broader NSE index declined 0.06% to 22,945.30 as investors took a pause after the recent drop, while the broader markets deepened losses due to expensive valuations.

RUPEE:

The Indian rupee closed at 86.95 against the U.S. dollar, down about 0.1% on the day, pressured by dollar demand spurred by the maturity of positions in the non-deliverable forwards (NDF) market and a decline in most of its regional peers.

GOVERNMENT BONDS:

The benchmark 10-year bond (IN071034G=CC) was quoted at 100.7375 rupees, with the yield down about 1 bp at 6.6834%, as investors, ahead of a market holiday, stayed on the sidelines awaiting fresh triggers scheduled for later this week.

OVERNIGHT INDEX SWAPS:

The one-year overnight index swap rate (INR1YMIBROIS=CC) was mostly unchanged at 6.3075%, while the five-year swap rate (INR5YMIBROIS=CC) was flat at 6.08%.

CALL MONEY:

India's overnight call money rate (INROND=) was at 6% and the overnight TREPS rate (INTREPTOT=TCCL) was at 6.5%.

data.title: Bulgaria's net FDI inflow drops 54% in 2024
data.description:
SOFIA (Bulgaria), February 18 (SeeNews) - The net inflow of foreign direct investment (FDI) into Bulgaria slumped by 54.4% year-on-year to nearly 1.498 billion euro ($1.569 billion) in 2024, the central bank said on Tuesday.

The net FDI inflow last year, calculated according to the directional principle, was equal to 1.5% of the country's forecast gross domestic product (GDP) for 2024, the Bulgarian National Bank (BNB) said in a monthly report, citing preliminary data.

In 2023, the net FDI inflow into Bulgaria represented 3.5% of the year's GDP.

In December alone, net FDI in Bulgaria posted an inflow of 78.7 million euro compared to an outflow of 114.1 million euro in the same month of 2023.

The largest net FDI inflow into Bulgaria in 2024, of 426.7 million euro, came from Austria, followed by Greece with 295.1 million euro and Italy with 245.9 million euro. Meanwhile, the biggest net outflow was directed to Luxembourg, at 436.7 million euro, and the U.S. with 378.9 million euro.

($ = 0.9548 euro)

data.title: Singapore's 2025 Budget Tackles Rising Costs, Trade Risks
data.description:
By Amanda Lee and Fabiana Negrin Ochoa

Singapore will beef up financial support for households and businesses as it looks to ease cost-of-living pressures in the Southeast Asian nation, and make the economy more competitive in the face of heightened global risks.

Speaking in parliament as he presented the budget for the fiscal year beginning in April, Prime Minister Lawrence Wong said the budget measures would tackle cost pressures for both businesses and individuals.

Wong, who is also the country's finance minister, said the city-state must also redouble efforts to grow its economy and stay dynamic in an increasingly contentious global environment.

"We cannot afford to stand still. Global economic competition is intensified, and many other countries and cities are pressing ahead. If we fail to stay competitive, we will be left behind," he said Tuesday.

With an election due by November and external risks rising, the budget's focus on costs on competitiveness had been widely anticipated. Singapore's economy made a strong recovery last year, with 4.4% growth eclipsing the 1.8% rate seen in 2023 as exports rebounded. But officials expect that momentum to slow this year, with U.S. trade policy uncertainties posing risks to the small, open economy.

Singapore runs a trade deficit with the U.S. and hasn't been directly targeted by tariffs under the Trump administration, but it is still vulnerable to indirect impacts from tariffs, economists say.

That said, the city-state's longstanding fiscal prudence and a bumper fiscal surplus mean it is well-positioned to prepare for the challenges ahead, Selena Ling, chief economist at OCBC, said in a recent note.

The prime minister said the government expects finish the 2024 fiscal year ending in March with a surplus of 6.4 billion Singapore dollars (US$4.77 billion), or 0.9% of gross domestic product. For the coming fiscal year, the government expects to have a similar fiscal position with a surplus of S$6.8 billion or 0.9% of GDP.

Singapore's fiscal position is expected to stay roughly balanced through 2030, Wong said, even with spending expected to go up to 20% of GDP.

Among the cost-easing measures mentioned in the speech was a 50% corporate income tax rebate. The prime minister also announced tax incentives for Singapore-based companies and fund managers that choose to list in Singapore, part of measures recommended by the central bank to strengthen the stock market's appeal. A tax incentive will also be introduced for fund managers that invest substantially in Singapore-listed equities.

Cash vouchers to help households cope with daily expenses and rebates for utilities costs also featured, with more support extended to families with children, pensioners and those under financial stress.

"While inflation is expected to ease further this year, prices remain high," the PM said, and Singaporeans are still adjusting to these new realities.

Wong pledged to keep public housing affordable and accessible for all, saying more supply would come on the market in the coming years.

Longer term, the best way to adjust to higher prices is to grow the economy and raise productivity, he added.

To that end, the budget will focus on technology & innovation, the enterprise ecosystem and infrastructure investment. Singapore's national productivity fund will be topped up by S$3 billion to enhance research-and-development in fields like biotechnology, and a new S$1 billion fund set up to finance local enterprises.

The budget's strong fiscal position and emphasis on building out Singapore's economic resilience will likely be seen as positive in the context of a potential global trade war, but uncertainty around U.S. policy will continue to cloud the outlook.

The prime minister warned that the escalating tussle between the U.S. and China will have "ripple effects that will inevitably draw in other countries, including Singapore," but that "despite the uncertainties, we can look ahead with a degree of confidence."

Write to Amanda Lee at amanda.lee@wsj.com and Fabiana Negrin Ochoa at fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Tuesday at 5 AM ET
data.description:
Investors Haven't Been This Pessimistic About Stocks Since 2023

Tumultuous policy changes, a potential trade war and sky-high stock prices have turned some investors bearish in 2025.

The S&P 500's race to a new record may tip it into a bigger pullback. Here's why.

S&P 500 looks poised to break out from the top of its multi-week trading range.

U.K. Wages Gather Pace, Complicating Bank of England's Task Ahead

Wages grew at a faster rate in the U.K. at the end of last year, posing a potential headache for the Bank of England after it cut interest rates again and warned of anemic economic growth this year.

Fed's Waller Says Policy Uncertainty Shouldn't Dissuade More Rate Cuts

Federal Reserve governor Christopher Waller said the central bank should cut interest rates further if inflation makes more progress back toward target.

Republican Senators Try to Curb Influence of Trump Tariff Hawk

The White House trade adviser has had a major influence on punitive levies while Trump's trade team awaits Senate confirmation.

RBA Joins Wave of Global Central Bank Easing

The Reserve Bank of Australia cut interest rates for the first time since 2020 on Tuesday, joining its international counterparts in the global easing cycle.

Defense Rally Pushes European Stocks to New Record

European defense stocks rose, buoyed by the prospect of higher military budgets, while global markets were largely subdued with the U.S. closed to mark Presidents Day.

This Stock Market May Not Be That Overpriced After All

A new version of the much-watched CAPE ratio indicates that yes, the market is overvalued-but not by nearly as much as feared.

The New Plan for Western Companies Is ABC: 'Anything But China'

With U.S.-China tensions soaring again, many tech businesses are accelerating moves to shift production out of China, offering opportunities for countries in Asia and Latin America to move up the value chain.

China Sends Message to Its Tech Leaders: We Need You

Chinese leader Xi Jinping signaled to top tech CEOs that he needed their help to deliver economic growth and self-sufficiency.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Tuesday at 5 AM ET
data.description:
Baidu Profit Doubles, Revenue Falls Less Than Expected

The Beijing-based internet company said Tuesday that fourth-quarter revenue fell 2.4%, better than analysts' estimates.

Anglo American Sells Nickel Business for Up to $500 Million

The sale, to a subsidiary of China's mining company MMG, is part of its effort to simplify operations, following a failed takeover bid from BHP last year.

China's Love Affair With Luxury Has Cooled

A sluggish economy, an austere political mood and the feeling among some consumers that pricey brands are passé have combined to end the boom that propelled the industry in recent years.

Tesla's Musk Is China's Bargaining Chip With Trump. It's Not Good for Investors.

The EV maker's CEO is a pawn in negotiations for the country's brewing trade war with the U.S.

Can Broadcom save Intel? What investors should know about latest deal rumors.

Broadcom reportedly has some interest in Intel's design business. But one analyst says "a lot seems like it needs to happen" for a deal to become reality.

BHP Sanguine on Tariff Threat to Canada Potash Bet

The world's biggest miner by market value is betting the fertilizer ingredient will be in high demand in the decades ahead as food consumption increases.

Occidental Petroleum Set to Report Earnings Drop. What's Dragging on the Buffett Favorite.

OXY has lost 21% over the past year. Weak crude prices are to blame.

Delta Regional Jet Crashes at Toronto Airport

The flight from Minneapolis suffered an incident while attempting to land. All 80 passengers and crew were evacuated, U.S. and Canadian authorities said.

Southwest Airlines to Slash Corporate Workforce in First Mass Layoff

The carrier is trying to cut costs after a battle with an activist investor last year.

DeepSeek Did Nvidia a Favor

Nvidia has big year ahead, but the selloff might set a better stage for its chip product transition.

data.title: Baidu profits slow in 'transformation year' but top estimates
data.description:
By Steve Goldstein

Baidu earnings beat analyst expectations

Chinese internet giant Baidu said its core business saw declining profits in the fourth quarter as online marketing revenue growth slowed even as its AI cloud business grew.

Baidu (BIDU) (HK:9888) said its non-GAAP earnings fell 13% to 6.71 billion yuan as revenue fell 2% to 34.12 billion yuan, or $4.68 billion.

Its adjusted U.S. dollar earnings per diluted American depository receipt were $2.63.

Analysts polled by FactSet expected adjusted earnings of $1.98 a share on sales of $4.6 billion.

"2024 marked a pivotal year in our ongoing transformation from an internet-centric to an AI-first business," said Robin Li, the chief executive and co-founder of Baidu, in a statement.

There were no visible Baidu representatives at a symposium Chinese President Xi Jinping held on Monday with private-sector leaders including Alibaba's Jack Ma.

Baidu shares slipped 2% in premarket trade, underperforming other Chinese internet stocks.

-Steve Goldstein

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: German ZEW Economic Sentiment Rises in January; Current Conditions Improve
data.description:
Germany's economic sentiment index increased to 26 points in February, from 10.3 points in January, research institute ZEW said Tuesday.

Analysts expected 20 points for the month.

Additionally, the current situation indicator came in at -88.5 points, up from the prior -90.4 points and expected -90.

data.title: Wheat Falls From 4-Month Highs
data.description:
Wheat futures dipped below $6.0 per bushel as the US dollar strengthened amid expectations that the Federal Reserve will delay interest rate cuts.

However, prices remained near a four-month high of $6.03, reached on February 14, as cold weather threatened crops in the Black Sea and US Plains regions, raising concerns about tightening supplies.

Additionally, there was some relief that US President Donald Trump has not yet announced tariffs on agricultural trade.

In February, the USDA unexpectedly cut domestic ending stocks for 2024/25 by 4 million bushels to 794 million, while global stocks fell by 1.3 million tons to 257.6 million, primarily due to downward revisions for China.

On the demand side, the USDA’s latest report showing wheat export sales surged 45% from the prior four-week average to 569,600 tonnes in the week ending February 6.

data.title: Europe Is Key to Lasting Ukraine Peace, French FM Insists — Market Talk
data.description: Only European pressure can bring about peace in Ukraine, France's top diplomat says as talks begin between the U.S. and Russia, without the presence of European or Ukrainian leaders. Europe's political leaders have gathered in Paris in response to their sidelining from peace talks, and are united over the need to ensure lasting peace for Ukraine following its full-scale invasion by neighboring Russia three years ago, Jean-Noël Barrot tells radio station France Info. Any peace agreement must dissuade Russia from any new aggression against its neighbors, the foreign minister says. And it is Europeans who can bring about security guarantees for Kiev, Barrot adds. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

data.title: Is now finally the time for telecoms?
data.description:
Key points:
  • STOXX 600 down 0.1%
  • US and Russia meet over Ukraine
  • Wall St returns from holiday
  • Nasdaq futures up 0.4%

IS NOW FINALLY THE TIME FOR TELECOMS?

Telecoms have been historical laggards and every now and then, hopes of a comeback resurface.

Only in April, the sector was at record lows relative to the broader European market . But this time around Swiss bank UBS is sensing a potential narrative shift.

"The sector is evolving into a total return trade, where yield is complemented by valuation catalysts and structural improvements in industry fundamentals," say UBS analysts.

For too long, the sector has been stereotyped as a high-yield and low-growth industry, plagued by capital intensity, pricing pressure and regulatory constraints.

In more detail, UBS's argument is based on four points.

  • Yield trade: "With ECB rate cuts ongoing, telecoms' stable, high dividends are becoming increasingly attractive versus bonds"

  • CapEx rolling over: "The long, expensive 5G and fibre buildout cycles are peaking"

  • M&A: "European regulators have pivoted from aggressive price controls to encouraging consolidation.. deals like Vodafone-Three mark a turning point in market structure"

  • Valuation: "European telecoms trade at a discount to US and Asian peers, but multiples haven't fully re-rated despite spread tightening"

So far this year, European telecoms (.SXKP) have risen by 8.1% while the STOXX 600 is up 9%.

*****

EARLIER ON LIVE MARKETS:

NEW RECORD HIGHS, WITH A BIT OF RED CLICK HERE

BEFORE THE BELL: EUROPE NEAR PEAKS, DEFENCE IN DEMAND CLICK HERE

ARMS STOCKS SOAR AHEAD OF UKRAINE PEACE TALKS CLICK HERE

data.title: German Investor Morale at 7-Month High
data.description:
The ZEW Indicator of Economic Sentiment for Germany climbed 15.7 points to +26 in February 2025, exceeding market expectations of +20 and reaching its highest level since July 2024.

It was driven in part by an improved outlook for the construction sector, following the European Central Bank’s recent interest rate cut and indications of further monetary easing.

data.title: Europe stocks come off record highs as IT stocks drag
data.description:
Key points:
  • STOXX 600 flat, defence index up 1.3%
  • Capgemini falls after reporting lower annual sales
  • French consumer prices rise by 1.8% YoY
  • Holiday Inn owner IHG down after 2024 results

European shares pulled back from their record highs on Tuesday, as Capgemini led IT stocks lower and rising bond yields put additional pressure on the market, while a rally in defence stocks began to lose momentum.

The pan-European STOXX 600 index gave up its early gains to trade flat at 555.40 points, as of 0920 GMT.

The aerospace and defence index (.SXPARO) rose 1.3%, with Italy's Leonardo up 2.3%, while Sweden's Saab AB rose 2.5% and Britain's BAE Systems gained 0.6%.

German arms manufacturer Rheinmetall ticked up 2.3%, while Thyssenkrupp , which is planning to spin off its warship division, advanced 3.1% after a nearly 20% surge on Monday.

The sector had surged 4.6% on Monday in its biggest one-day rise since Russia's invasion of Ukraine in February 2022. The surge came after European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said the commission would propose exempting defence spending from EU government spending limits.

U.S. and Russian officials met in Riyadh for the highest-level talks to date between the two former Cold War foes on ending the war in Ukraine.

"When you see such a strong momentum, you tend to see a pullback just to stabilise that momentum... you may see some profit-taking," said Daniela Hathorn, senior market analyst at Capital.com.

Keeping a lid on the market's gains, Capgemini slid 9% after the French IT consulting giant reported a 2% annual decline in sales, though it surpassed expectations. The technology sector lost (.SX8P) 1.1%.

Elevated bond yields also weighed on the broader market as investors expected increased European bond issuance to fund defence spending. The yield on the German 10-year benchmark (DE10YT=RR) was near a three-week high.

Higher yields benefited the banking sector (.SX7P), which rose 0.7%, while rate-sensitive real estate stocks (.SX86P) fell by 0.5%.

Utilities (.SX6P), often seen as bond proxies, were among the largest drags with a decline of 0.7%. Enagas dropped 2.9% after the Spanish gas grid operator anticipated a fall in core earnings this year.

On the data front, consumer prices in France rose by 1.8% year-on-year in January, aligning with analysts' expectations and the preliminary reading.

In the UK, pay growth accelerated in late 2024, highlighting why the Bank of England has been cautious about cutting interest rates despite a generally weak economy.

German investor morale improved more than expected in February.

Among other stocks, IHG , the owner of Holiday Inn, lost 3.1% following the announcement of its 2024 results.

Antofagasta gained 2.7% after the miner's annual core profit rose 11%.

Glencore ticked up 1.3% after Morgan Stanley upgraded its rating to "overweight" from "equal weight".

data.title: Capgemini Shares Fall on Cautious Outlook — Update
data.description:
By Najat Kantouar

Capgemini shares fell after the French company said it remains cautious on its outlook and reported lower revenue as customers were slow to make spending decisions.

The consulting and technology group said Tuesday that it is confronting an uncertain environment, particularly in the manufacturing sector and in Europe. The company forecast its revenue performance excluding currency movements for the first half of 2025 would be in line with the 1.1% decline it posted for last year's fourth quarter.

In European morning trading, shares in Capgemini were down about 9.3%. If maintained until close, this would represent the stock's biggest one-day percentage drop since March 2020, at the outbreak of the pandemic. Shares have risen 6.3% year to date.

Consulting companies like Capgemini, Accenture and Infosys have been hurt by sluggish client spending over the past few quarters. Capgemini said the environment proved weaker than initially anticipated last year, but that a shift in its portfolio toward higher-value services helped it protect profitability.

The company said it anticipates full-year revenue at constant currency rates to range from a 2% fall to a 2% rise, and targets an operating margin between 13.3% to 13.5%.

Capgemini's full-year guidance looks conservative, Jefferies analysts wrote in a note. "Having misjudged the speed of market recovery in 2024, we can understand the rationale for starting 2025 on a more conservative footing," the analysts said.

For 2024, group posted a 0.5% rise in net profit to 1.67 billion euros, equivalent to $1.75 billion, from 1.66 billion euros for the same period a year earlier. This compared with market consensus of 1.69 billion euros based on 13 analysts taken from FactSet.

Operating profit increased to 2.36 billion euros from 2.35 billion euros, while operating margin was flat at 13.3%.

Revenue fell to 22.10 billion euros from 22.52 billion euros. The consensus stood at 22.05 billion euros, according to FactSet-provided estimates.

In the fourth quarter, revenue totaled 5.58 billion euros, falling 1.1% on a constant-currency basis. Capgemini said revenue trends gradually improved throughout the year after bottoming out in the first quarter.

Bookings rose 1.9% in the fourth quarter, but the company said clients are still taking a long time to make decisions. Generative artificial intelligence services accounted for 5% of the company's bookings in the quarter, it said.

The group declared a dividend of 3.40 euros a share, flat from a year earlier.

Write to Najat Kantouar at najat.kantouar@wsj.com

data.title: Argeo ASA: New share capital registered
data.description:

Reference is made to the stock exchange release fromArgeo ASA ("Argeo" or the "Company") (Euronext Oslo Børs: ARGEO) published on12February 2025 announcing the completion of a private placement of new sharesinthe Company, raising gross proceeds of NOK 150 million (the "PrivatePlacement").

The board of directors resolved on 12 February 2025 to issue the sharesallocated in Tranche 1 of the Private Placement pursuant to an authorisationgranted to the board by the general meeting in the Company held on 6 June2024.

The share capital increase related to Tranche 1 of the Private Placement hastoday been registered with the Norwegian Register of Business Enterprises. Thenew share capital of the Company is NOK 26,650,674.50 divided into 53,301,349shares, each with nominal value of NOK 0.50.

For more information, please contact:

Trond Figenschou Crantz, CEOEmail: trond.crantz@argeo.noPhone: +47 976 37 273

About Argeo ASAArgeo is a comprehensive subsea service provider, operating across three majorsectors: Oil & Gas, Marine Minerals, and Renewables. The Company offers auniquepackage that integrates robust vessels, state-of-the-art autonomous underwatervehicles (AUVs), advanced sensors, digital imaging technology, and anintuitivedigital platform designed to collect and visualize complex data. With their own vessels and high-performance AUVs, Argeo delivers fast andflexible full-lifecycle services, including survey, inspection, maintenance,andrepair, aimed at improving efficiency and reducing the carbon footprint forglobal, industry-leading clients.

Founded in 2020, Argeo has established offices in Asker (Oslo), Edinburgh,Houston, and Rio de Janeiro.

data.title: Nigeria Inflation Rate Falls to 24.48% After CPI Rebasement
data.description:
Nigeria's inflation rate was at 24.48% in January 2025, compared to December’s 34.80%, following the rebasing of the Consumer Price Index (CPI) by the country's statistical office to more accurately reflect changes in consumption patterns.

Rebased food inflation, which constitutes more than 50% of Nigeria’s old inflation basket, was 26.08% year-on-year in January, down from 39.84% in December.

However, the National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) clarified that this decline in the rebased inflation rate does not indicate a decrease in the general price level.

The agency explained that the primary factor behind the decline was the base year (2024) being closer to the current period.

data.title: KKR Acquires Stake in Employment Hero From SEEK Investments
data.description:
KKR KKR said Monday that funds it manages have acquired a stake in Employment Hero from SEEK Investments, manager of the SEEK Growth Fund, which continues to retain a stake in Employment Hero.

Financial terms were not disclosed.

Australia's Employment Hero provides human resources management tools to more than 300,000 small and medium-sized organizations worldwide, KKR said.

The deal is expected to close in Q1, KKR said.

data.title: Palm Oil Ends Lower Amid Profit-Taking, Demand Concerns — Market Talk
data.description: Palm oil ended lower amid likely profit-taking after previous session's gains and concerns over weak export demand, according to Kenanga Futures says in a research note. However, the tightening palm oil stocks in Malaysia could provide support for the vegetable oil's prices, it adds. Meanwhile, Indonesia's new regulation requiring exporters of resources--excluding oil and gas--to keep all export proceeds locally for at least a year starting in March could pose challenges for Indonesian palm oil exporters, Phillip Nova analyst Lim Tai An says in a note. The move could benefit Malaysian exporters, who are poised to compete for more market share and support palm oil prices, he adds. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for May delivery was 34 ringgit lower at 4,508 ringgit a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Eurazeo Invests in MPC Capital's Offshore Service Vessel Platform
data.description:
French investment group Eurazeo and a European family office led the funding round for MPC Münchmeyer Petersen Capital's , or MPC Capital's, planned offshore service vessels platform.

The platform aims to develop, construct, and manage vessels that will operate in offshore wind farms. The project is part of a joint venture between O.S. Energy and the German investment and asset manager, according to a Tuesday release.

Eurazeo carried out its equity investment through its Eurazeo Transition Infrastructure Fund.

data.title: U.K. Sells 1.75 Billion Pounds in October 2063 Gilt
data.description:
By Miriam Mukuru

The U.K's Debt Management Office sold 1.75 billion pounds ($2.21 billion) in October 2063-dated gilts at an auction on Tuesday, it said.

The following are results of the auction, with amounts in pounds. Figures in brackets are data from the previous auction, held on June 4, 2024.

Issue              4.0% October 22 2063 gilt 
Amount on offer    1.75  bln 
Bids received      4.90  bln 
Bids accepted      1.75  bln 
Bid-to-cover ratio 2.80    (3.10) 
Average yield      5.076%  (4.557%) 
Average price      81.84   (89.85) 
Maximum yield      5.079%  (4.570%) 
Settlement date    February 19, 2025

Write to Miriam Mukuru at miriam.mukuru@wsj.com

data.title: Delta Air Lines Stock Falls After Regional Plane Flips Over. What to Know. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

A Delta Air Lines flight operated by a regional jet subsidiary dramatically flipped over on landing at Pearson International Airport in Toronto, Canada.

The Delta Connection flight 4819 was operated by Delta subsidiary Endeavor Air and originated from Minneapolis-St. Paul International Airport. The incident happened at about 2:15 p.m. Eastern time on Monday.

"Initial reports indicate there are no fatalities, and 18 customers with injuries have been transported to area hospitals," reads part of a statement from Delta. "Our primary focus is taking care of those impacted."

The aircraft involved was a Bombardier CRJ 900. Bombardier introduced the 900 model in the early 2000s, but the company sold its regional jet business to Mitsubishi Heavy Industries in 2020.

The Delta incident comes weeks after an American Airlines regional flight crashed with an Army helicopter, tragically killing everyone involved.

Coming into Tuesday's trading, American Airlines stock was down about 8% since the collision. The S&P 500 was up about 1% over the same span.

The plane involved in the American Airlines crash was a Bombardier CRJ 700. There hasn't been any information putting the plane at fault in either incident, and both investigations are ongoing.

The National Transportation Safety Board held a Feb. 14 briefing about the Jan. 29 American Airlines crash. There are open questions about the altitude of the helicopter and if the helicopter crew received air traffic updates.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Yinson Repurchases Shares Worth MYR5 Million
data.description:
Yinson bought back 2.3 million shares for around 5.1 million ringgit, according to a Tuesday disclosure with Bursa Malaysia.

The shares were repurchased at between 2.210 ringgit and 2.270 ringgit apiece, the energy infrastructure and technology company said.
